title: How Much Do Dental Implants Cost in Texas? (2026)
seoTitle: Dental Implants Cost in Texas (2026 Guide)
seoDescription: Honest 2026 pricing for dental implants in Texas. Single tooth, multiple teeth, and All-on-4 ranges, plus financing options that work.
author: Sonria Dentista clinical team
summary: A clear breakdown of what dental implants actually cost in Texas in 2026, including single-tooth, multi-tooth, and full-arch ranges. We cover financing, insurance, and how to avoid surprise fees.

body.text: If you have searched for dental implants cost in Texas, you have probably seen wildly different numbers. One ad says $899. Another quotes $7,000. Both can be technically true, depending on what is included. This guide walks through what dental implants really cost in Texas in 2026, what drives the price up or down, and how families pay for them without taking on more debt than they can handle.

We are Sonria Dentista, a bilingual family practice with four locations across North and Northeast Texas. Our team places hundreds of implants every year, so the numbers below come from real cases, not from a marketing brochure.
How much do dental implants cost in Texas in 2026?

For a standard single-tooth implant in Texas in 2026, expect a total cost between $1,500 and $6,000. That wide range exists because a complete implant has three parts and they are not always priced together.

items: The implant itself: the titanium post that goes into the jawbone, usually $1,000 to $3,000.
The abutment: the connector piece that sits on top of the implant, usually $300 to $700.
The crown: the visible ceramic tooth that snaps onto the abutment, usually $1,000 to $2,500.

body.text: When a clinic advertises an implant for $899, that price almost always covers the post only. The abutment, the crown, the imaging, the surgery, and the follow-up visits are billed separately. By the time the tooth is in your mouth, you may be at $4,500 or more. That is not a scam, but it is the most common reason patients feel misled.

body.title: What we charge
body.text: At Sonria, a complete single-tooth implant is $2,499 all-inclusive. That covers the titanium post, the abutment, and the ceramic crown. No surprise add-ons unless you need a bone graft.
What changes the price of a dental implant?
Two patients in the same chair on the same day can pay very different amounts. The drivers are mostly clinical:
Bone density and grafting

If you lost a tooth years ago, your jawbone in that spot has likely shrunk. Without enough bone to anchor the implant, you may need a bone graft first. A small graft adds $300 to $800. A sinus lift, used for upper back teeth, adds $1,500 to $2,500. The graft has to heal before the implant goes in, which can add three to six months to the timeline.
Tooth extraction
If the failing tooth is still in your mouth, the extraction is part of the process. A simple extraction is $150 to $300. A surgical extraction (broken tooth, deep root) is $300 to $650.
Imaging and planning
A 3D CBCT scan ($250 to $400) is essential for safe implant placement. Some clinics fold this into the implant price. Others charge separately. Always ask.
Where the work is done

A general dentist who places implants in-office often charges less than a specialist. Both are qualified for routine cases. For complex cases (severe bone loss, full-mouth reconstruction), a specialist is worth the extra cost.
How much do multiple implants cost?
If you are missing several teeth, you do not always need one implant per tooth. There are smarter, cheaper options:

items: Implant-supported bridge: 2 implants holding 3 to 4 fake teeth. Total cost $5,500 to $9,000.
Implant-supported partial denture: 2 to 4 implants holding a removable partial. $7,000 to $14,000.
All-on-4: 4 implants per arch holding a full set of fixed teeth. $20,000 to $35,000 per arch at most clinics. We charge $15,999 per arch all-inclusive.
All-on-6 or full mouth: $40,000 to $60,000 for both upper and lower arches at most clinics.

body.text: If you have lost most of your teeth, All-on-4 is usually the most cost-effective path. You walk out the same day with fixed teeth on four implants instead of paying for six or eight separate ones.
Does insurance cover dental implants?
Most dental PPO plans cover implants partially. The two common patterns:

items: Some plans cover 50% of the implant up to a yearly maximum (often $1,000 to $2,500).
Some plans cover only the crown portion, treating the implant post as not covered.
Almost all plans have a waiting period (6 to 12 months) before they pay for major work.

body.text: Before you sign anything, have the dentist verify your benefits in writing. We do this for free at Sonria. The verification tells you exactly what your plan will pay and what you owe out of pocket. If you have a yearly maximum of $1,500 and the implant runs $2,499, your share would be around $1,000 plus whatever your plan does not cover.
How do most people actually pay for implants?
Almost no one pays for a full-arch case in cash. The most common payment paths in 2026:
Medical credit cards

Cards like CareCredit are designed for healthcare costs. They typically offer six to twenty-four months of zero-interest financing if you qualify. The catch: if you do not pay the full balance by the end of the promo period, deferred interest kicks in retroactively. Always pay it off on time.
In-house dental payment plans

Many dental offices, including ours, offer fixed monthly payments without an aggressive credit check. At Sonria, in-house plans run six to twelve months, usually $200 to $500 per month for a single implant.
Personal loans

For larger cases (All-on-4, full mouth), some patients take a personal loan from a credit union. Rates are usually 8% to 14% APR. Compare against medical credit before signing, since a no-interest medical card is almost always cheaper if you can pay it off in the promo window.
FSA and HSA accounts
If you have a health savings account or flexible spending account, dental implants are an eligible expense. You pay with pre-tax dollars, which effectively gives you a 22% to 30% discount.

body.title: Watch out for
body.text: Any clinic that pressures you to sign a financing contract the same day as the consult. A real practice gives you the price in writing and a few days to think about it.
What about cheap dental implants in Mexico?

We get this question every week. Implants in Monterrey or Los Algodones can be 40% to 60% cheaper than the same procedure in Texas. For a healthy patient with a single tooth and a flexible schedule, this can work. But two warnings:

items: If something goes wrong (failed integration, infection, fractured crown), the follow-up has to happen back in Mexico, or you pay a Texas dentist a premium to fix another clinic's work.
Quality varies more than in the US. Some Mexico clinics are excellent. Others use brands of implants that are not common in the US, making future repair harder.

body.text: If you go this route, ask which implant brand they use (look for Straumann, Nobel Biocare, or other globally recognized brands), and confirm what happens if you have a problem six months after you fly home.
How long do dental implants last?

A well-placed implant lasts 25 years or more. The crown on top is the part that wears, and most crowns last 10 to 15 years before needing replacement. Studies on 20-year implant survival rates show 90% to 95% are still in place. The main causes of failure are smoking, uncontrolled diabetes, poor home hygiene, or grinding without a night guard.
What to ask before you book
If you are getting quotes from different offices, ask the same five questions at each one:

items: Is this price all-inclusive (post, abutment, crown, X-rays, follow-ups)?
Does it include a 3D CBCT scan?
What happens if I need a bone graft? How much extra?
What brand of implant do you use?
What is your warranty if the implant fails in the first 5 years?

body.text: A real practice will answer all five in writing. If they hedge or change the subject, keep shopping.
Frequently asked questions
Are dental implants worth it compared to a bridge or a denture?

For one or two missing teeth, an implant costs more upfront ($2,500 to $5,000 vs $1,500 to $2,500 for a bridge), but it lasts twice as long and does not damage neighboring teeth. Over 20 years, an implant is usually cheaper. For multiple missing teeth, dentures cost less upfront ($1,500 to $4,000) but most patients hate them. Implant-supported options are far more comfortable and stable.
How long does the whole process take from first visit to final tooth?

For a standard single implant: three to six months. The implant placement itself is one visit. Then you wait three to six months for the bone to grow around the post (osseointegration). Then we place the final crown. If you need a graft first, add another three to four months.
Does it hurt?

Less than most people expect. Local anesthesia handles the placement itself. After the surgery, most patients use ibuprofen for one or two days and return to work the next morning. A graft adds a bit more soreness but rarely requires anything stronger than over-the-counter pain relief.
Can I get an implant if I have low bone or have been missing the tooth for years?
Usually yes, with a bone graft. The 3D scan tells us exactly how much bone you have and what kind of graft would work. Very few patients are completely ineligible for implants in 2026.
Talk to a real dentist before deciding

Every implant case is different. The honest answer to what does it cost only comes after a real exam. We do free implant consultations at all four Sonria offices, with a 3D scan and a written, all-inclusive price. Call (682) 284-1120 to book.
